But in C++, the 'find' function can be used to check if a substring exists.

So, in code:

#include <string>

using namespace std;

class Solution {
public:
    int repeatedStringMatch(string a, string b) {
        // Check if b is already a substring of a
        if (a.find(b) != string::npos) {
            return 1;
        }
        
        int len_a = a.size();
        int len_b = b.size();
        
        // Compute k_min
        int k_min = (len_b + len_a - 1) / len_a;
        
        // Create s with k_min repetitions
        string s;
        for (int i = 0; i < k_min; ++i) {
            s += a;
        }
        if (s.find(b) != string::npos) {
            return k_min;
        }
        
        // Create s with k_min +1 repetitions
        s += a;
        if (s.find(b) != string::npos) {
            return k_min + 1;
        }
        
        // If not found in either case
        return -1;
    }
};

solution: 
```cpp
#include <string>

using namespace std;

class Solution {
public:
    int repeatedStringMatch(string a, string b) {
        if (a.find(b) != string::npos) {
            return 1;
        }
        
        int len_a = a.size();
        int len_b = b.size();
        
        int k_min = (len_b + len_a - 1) / len_a;
        
        string s;
        for (int i = 0; i < k_min; ++i) {
            s += a;
        }
        if (s.find(b) != string::npos) {
            return k_min;
        }
        
        s += a;
        if (s.find(b) != string::npos) {
            return k_min + 1;
        }
        
        return -1;
    }
};
```

efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(k_min * len_a + len_b)
space complexity: O(k_min * len_a)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution efficiently checks the minimal repetitions needed by leveraging substring searches and avoids unnecessary string construction beyond the required repetitions.
